---
title: "Power and Sample Size: Designing a Test That Can Actually Find Something"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20241104)

NULLC  = "#2b3a67"     # the null world
ALTC   = "#0a7d4d"     # the alternative world
REJECT = "#c1121f"     # rejection region / critical value
TYPE2  = "#d6791f"     # Type II / beta
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas
ACCENT = "#6a2c91"     # observed / highlights

def normal_pdf(t, mu=0.0, sigma=1.0):
    """The Gaussian density — used to draw null and alternative sampling distributions."""
    return np.exp(-0.5 * ((t - mu) / sigma) ** 2) / (sigma * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the *design* article of the **Hypothesis
> Testing** movement (D) of the Statistical Inference track. It assumes two earlier
> articles. From [INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*] it takes the entire decision
> frame: the null $H_0$ and alternative $H_1$, the **two ways to be wrong** (Type I rate
> $\alpha$ and Type II rate $\beta$), and the **power** $1-\beta$ defined as a probability
> *under a specified alternative*. From [INFR-D2 *The Workhorse Tests*] it takes the
> concrete z- and t-test statistics whose power we now compute. The only thing imported
> from outside the track is the standard-Normal CDF $\Phi$ and its quantiles $z_\alpha$,
> built in the Probability Foundations track ([PROB-11]), used by name. Where [INFR-D1]
> defined power and [INFR-D2] ran tests *after* the data arrived, this article asks the
> question that comes *first*: **before collecting anything, how large a sample do we need
> for the test to stand a real chance of detecting an effect worth detecting?** It is the
> disciplined answer to the significance-vs-importance trap [INFR-D1] flagged. It **sets
> up** [INFR-D5 *Multiple Comparisons*] (power under multiplicity) and informs every
> experimental design downstream, in this track and in Regression.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A hypothesis test that cannot detect the effect it is looking for is theater: it goes
through the motions, returns "not significant," and tells you nothing. **Power** is the
probability a test *does* detect a real effect of a given size, and it is a deterministic
function of four quantities — the effect size, the sample size $n$, the significance level
$\alpha$, and the noise. This article derives that function explicitly for the z- and
t-test, every step from the definition in [INFR-D1]. We compute the **power curve**, invert
it to get the **sample-size formula** $n = (z_\alpha + z_\beta)^2/\delta^2$, and read off
the **minimum detectable effect** for a fixed budget. We separate **effect size** from
**statistical significance** with honest examples — a trivially small effect made
"significant" by brute sample size, and a real effect missed because the study was too
small. The article's edge-of-applicability case is the most consequential confusion in
applied statistics: the difference between **"underpowered"** and **"no effect,"** and the
file-drawer and p-hacking damage that follows from getting it wrong. The single claim it
defends is that **power is something you buy before the experiment, not something you
discover after** — and that a non-significant result from an underpowered study is not
evidence of absence, it is absence of evidence.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The **power** of a test is the probability it correctly rejects a false null:
$$
\text{power} = 1-\beta = P(\text{reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ true, effect size } \delta).
$$
It is **not** a free-floating number — it is pinned down by exactly four levers, and fixing
any three determines the fourth:

1. the **effect size** $\delta$ — how far the truth sits from the null, *in units of noise*;
2. the **sample size** $n$ — how much data you collect;
3. the **significance level** $\alpha$ — your tolerated false-alarm rate;
4. the **variance** $\sigma^2$ — how noisy a single observation is.

For the one-sample z-test these combine into one formula. The power is
$$
1-\beta = \Phi\!\left(\sqrt{n}\,\frac{\delta}{\sigma} - z_\alpha\right),
$$
and inverting it to hit a target power gives the **sample-size formula**
$$
n = \frac{(z_\alpha + z_\beta)^2\,\sigma^2}{\delta^2}.
$$
The one fact to carry: **$n$ grows like $1/\delta^2$.** Halving the effect you want to
detect *quadruples* the sample you need. Power is bought, in advance, at a known price.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. Two ideas inside it are the ones an examiner will push
on. First: **power is computed in the alternative world**, not the null — the conditioning
bar reads "$\mid H_1$ true." This is the exact mirror of [INFR-D1]'s $\alpha$, which lived
under $H_0$; you cannot draw the alternative's sampling distribution until you commit to
*how big* the effect is, which is why $\delta$ must be named before any power exists.
Second: **the $1/\delta^2$ scaling is the whole economics of experiment design.** It is why
small effects demand enormous studies, why a "null result" from a tiny sample is worthless,
and why power must be planned, never inspected after the fact.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced power analysis {#sec-why}

Hold one question in view for the whole article: **how do we keep a test from failing
silently?** A test can be wrong in two ways ([INFR-D1]), and the apparatus there spent all
its discipline on *one* of them. We fix $\alpha$ in advance, we calibrate the rejection
region so a true null fires falsely only $5\%$ of the time, we prove the p-value is uniform
under the null. Every guarantee is about the **null world**. Nothing in [INFR-D1] forces
the test to *succeed* when there genuinely is an effect.

That asymmetry is dangerous because of how the two failures *feel*. A Type I error is loud:
you announce a discovery, others try to build on it, and the false alarm eventually surfaces
when nobody can replicate it. A Type II error is silent: you run the study, get "$p=0.31$,"
shrug, and conclude "no effect" — when in fact there *was* an effect and your study was
simply too small to see it. The test gave you exactly the wrong answer and left no
fingerprint. @sec-effect-vs-sig and @sec-underpowered are about precisely this silent
failure, which is the costliest mistake in applied work.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why isn't controlling $\alpha$ enough? Why do we need power at all?"* Because $\alpha$
only protects you against finding things that aren't there; it does nothing to ensure you
find the things that are. A test with $\alpha=0.05$ and power $0.10$ is perfectly valid —
it controls false alarms — and almost useless, because it misses $90\%$ of real effects.
Worse, the few effects it does catch are systematically *overestimated* (only the lucky,
large sample draws clear the bar — the "winner's curse" of @sec-underpowered). Controlling
$\alpha$ without controlling $\beta$ is buying a smoke detector that you have wired to never
go off: no false alarms, no fire detection either.
:::

## A one-paragraph history

Power is the **Neyman–Pearson** half of testing ([INFR-D1]'s second parent). Fisher's
significance testing had no formal alternative and hence no notion of power; you computed a
p-value and stopped. Jerzy Neyman and Egon Pearson, recasting testing as a decision between
two hypotheses around 1933, introduced $\beta$ and power as the quantity a good test should
*maximize* for a fixed $\alpha$ — and their lemma (the subject of [INFR-D3]) identifies the
most powerful test of all. The practical engineering of power — *choosing $n$ to hit a
target power before running the study* — was systematized by **Jacob Cohen** in the 1960s,
whose work on standardized effect sizes and power tables turned "how big a sample do I need"
from guesswork into a calculation. This article rebuilds Cohen's calculation from the
[INFR-D1] definition, every step.

# Intuition before symbols: pulling the two worlds apart {#sec-intuition}

[INFR-D1]'s central picture was **two overlapping worlds**: the null distribution of the
test statistic centered at its no-effect value, and the alternative distribution shifted by
the effect. We drew one vertical line — the critical value $c$ — and rejected when the
statistic landed past it. The null mass beyond $c$ was $\alpha$; the alternative mass
*short* of $c$ was $\beta$; the alternative mass *beyond* $c$ was the power.

Power analysis is the study of **how that overlap shrinks**. Anything that pulls the two
curves apart relative to their own width pushes more of the alternative curve past the line,
raising power. There are exactly three ways to do it, and @fig-power-levers shows each:

- **Bigger effect $\delta$** — slides the alternative curve further right. (Usually not
  under your control: the effect is whatever nature made it.)
- **More data $n$** — narrows *both* curves, because the sampling distribution of a mean has
  spread $\sigma/\sqrt n$. The centers stay put but the curves get skinny, so they stop
  overlapping. (Your main lever.)
- **Looser $\alpha$** — slides the line $c$ left, scooping more of the alternative curve
  into the rejection region. (Cheap, but it buys power by accepting more false alarms — a
  trade, not a gift.)

```{python}
#| label: fig-power-levers
#| fig-cap: 'The three levers of power, each shrinking the overlap between the null world (blue) and the alternative world (green). The red dashed line is the critical value $c$; the green-shaded area beyond it is the power. **Left:** the baseline. **Middle:** doubling the effect size $\delta$ slides the alternative curve right, sweeping far more of it past $c$. **Right:** quadrupling $n$ leaves the centers fixed but narrows both curves (spread $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$), pulling them apart relative to their width. Both maneuvers raise power; only $n$ is reliably under the experimenter''s control.'
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(10.2, 3.4), sharey=True)

def draw_worlds(ax, shift, sd, title):
    t = np.linspace(-4, 8, 800)
    c = 1.645 * sd            # critical value scales with the SE
    npdf = normal_pdf(t, 0.0, sd)
    apdf = normal_pdf(t, shift, sd)
    ax.plot(t, npdf, color=NULLC, lw=1.8)
    ax.plot(t, apdf, color=ALTC, lw=1.8)
    m = t >= c
    ax.fill_between(t[m], apdf[m], color=ALTC, alpha=0.35)
    ax.axvline(c, color=REJECT, ls="--", lw=1.3)
    pw = 1 - stats.norm.cdf((c - shift) / sd)
    ax.set_title(f"{title}\npower $\\approx$ {pw:.2f}", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlim(-4, 8); ax.set_xlabel("test statistic")

draw_worlds(axes[0], shift=2.0, sd=1.0, title="baseline")
draw_worlds(axes[1], shift=4.0, sd=1.0, title="effect $\\delta$ doubled")
draw_worlds(axes[2], shift=2.0, sd=0.5, title="$n$ quadrupled ($\\sigma/\\sqrt{n}$ halved)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("density")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Notice in the right panel that *both* curves got skinnier while their centers held still:
that is the entire mechanism by which more data buys power. The effect's *size* did not
change; the **ruler got finer**, so the same gap now reads as many standard errors. This is
the benign mirror image of [INFR-D1]'s Trap 3 (significance is not importance): there, a
fine ruler made a trivial effect look significant; here, the same fine ruler lets a real
effect clear the bar. The ruler is honest in both cases — it is the *interpretation* that
must stay disciplined.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The power function, defined {#sec-power-fn}

We work the one-sample, known-variance z-test of [INFR-D1] @sec-build — the cleanest case,
which isolates the logic before [INFR-D2]'s t-correction muddies it. We have
$X_1,\dots,X_n$ i.i.d. with unknown mean $\mu$ and known variance $\sigma^2$, and we test

$$
H_0:\ \mu = \mu_0 \qquad\text{versus}\qquad H_1:\ \mu > \mu_0
$$

at level $\alpha$, rejecting when the statistic $Z=(\bar X-\mu_0)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$ exceeds
$z_\alpha$ (the upper-$\alpha$ Normal quantile, with $P(\mathcal N(0,1)>z_\alpha)=\alpha$).

::: {#def-power name="Power function"}
The **power function** of a test is the probability of
rejecting $H_0$, viewed as a function of the *true* parameter value $\mu$:
$$
\pi(\mu) \;=\; P\bigl(\text{reject } H_0 \,\big|\, \text{true mean} = \mu\bigr).
$$
At $\mu=\mu_0$ it returns the Type I rate, $\pi(\mu_0)=\alpha$; at any $\mu$ in the
alternative it returns the power against *that* specific alternative, $\pi(\mu)=1-\beta(\mu)$.
:::

The power function is one object that contains everything: evaluated under the null it is
$\alpha$, evaluated under any alternative it is the power there. The "power" people quote is
$\pi$ at the one alternative they care about. Let us compute $\pi(\mu)$ outright.

## Deriving the power formula, every step {#sec-derive}

The rejection event is $Z \ge z_\alpha$, i.e.

$$
\frac{\bar X - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt n} \;\ge\; z_\alpha .
$$

We want its probability *when the true mean is some $\mu$* (possibly $\neq\mu_0$). The
obstacle is that the statistic is standardized around the *null* value $\mu_0$, but the data
actually scatter around the *true* value $\mu$. So under the truth, $Z$ is **not** standard
Normal — that was only its null distribution. We find its true distribution by
re-standardizing.

When the true mean is $\mu$, [INFR-A1] gives $\mathbb E[\bar X]=\mu$ and
$\operatorname{Var}(\bar X)=\sigma^2/n$, so the genuinely standard-Normal quantity is
$(\bar X-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$, **not** the one with $\mu_0$. Rewrite the statistic by
adding and subtracting the true mean inside the numerator:

$$
Z = \frac{\bar X - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt n}
  = \frac{(\bar X - \mu) + (\mu - \mu_0)}{\sigma/\sqrt n}
  = \underbrace{\frac{\bar X - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}}_{=\,W\,\sim\,\mathcal N(0,1)}
    \;+\; \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt n} .
$$

The first piece $W$ is exactly standard Normal under the truth; the second piece is a
**constant shift** — the true mean's distance from the null, measured in standard errors.
Call that shift

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt n} \;=\; \sqrt n\,\frac{\mu-\mu_0}{\sigma},
$$ {#eq-ncp}

the **noncentrality** — it is how far the alternative curve sits from the null curve, in
@fig-power-levers's units. So under the truth, $Z = W + \lambda$ is Normal with mean
$\lambda$ and variance $1$: the alternative curve is the null curve slid right by $\lambda$.

Now compute the power directly. The test rejects when $Z\ge z_\alpha$, i.e. when
$W+\lambda\ge z_\alpha$, i.e. when $W \ge z_\alpha - \lambda$:

$$
\pi(\mu) = P(Z \ge z_\alpha)
         = P(W + \lambda \ge z_\alpha)
         = P(W \ge z_\alpha - \lambda).
$$

Since $W\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ ($\Phi$ is the standard-Normal CDF, $\Phi(a)=P(W\le a)$), the
upper-tail probability is $P(W\ge a)=1-\Phi(a)$ — total mass is $1$, so the mass *above* $a$
is whatever is not *at or below* it. We also use $1-\Phi(a)=\Phi(-a)$. This is not magic: the
standard-Normal density is **even**, $\varphi(w)=\varphi(-w)$ (the bell is mirror-symmetric
about $0$, since $w$ enters only as $w^2$), so the area to the right of $a$ equals the area to
the left of $-a$ — i.e. $P(W\ge a)=P(W\le -a)=\Phi(-a)$. Apply this with
$a=z_\alpha-\lambda$:

$$
\boxed{\;\pi(\mu) \;=\; \Phi\bigl(\lambda - z_\alpha\bigr)
       \;=\; \Phi\!\left(\sqrt n\,\frac{\mu-\mu_0}{\sigma} - z_\alpha\right).\;}
$$ {#eq-power}

Read @eq-power slowly, because it is the engine of the whole article. The power rises
through $\Phi$ — a CDF, so monotone increasing — as its argument grows. The argument grows
when:

- $\mu-\mu_0$ grows (bigger true effect): the alternative is further from the null;
- $n$ grows (more data): the $\sqrt n$ multiplies the standardized effect;
- $\sigma$ shrinks (less noise): the effect is larger relative to the spread;
- $z_\alpha$ shrinks, i.e. $\alpha$ grows (looser test): the bar is lower.

These are exactly the four levers of the Core, now read off a single formula. A sanity
check: at $\mu=\mu_0$ the standardized effect is zero, so $\pi(\mu_0)=\Phi(-z_\alpha)=\alpha$
— the power function returns the Type I rate under the null, as @def-power demanded.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where did the noncentrality come from, and why is it $\sqrt n$ and not $n$?"* The shift
$\lambda$ measures the true mean's distance from the null *in units of the standard error*
$\sigma/\sqrt n$. Because the standard error shrinks like $1/\sqrt n$ ([INFR-A1] — averaging
$n$ values divides the spread by $\sqrt n$, not by $n$), the *same* physical gap $\mu-\mu_0$
reads as $\sqrt n$ times more standard errors as $n$ grows. That $\sqrt n$ is the [INFR-A2]
CLT scaling resurfacing: estimation error falls like $1/\sqrt n$, so detectable signal rises
like $\sqrt n$. It is also the root of the $1/\delta^2$ sample-size law: detection depends on
$\sqrt n\,\delta$, so to hold detection fixed while halving $\delta$ you must quadruple $n$.
:::

## The standardized effect size {#sec-effectsize-def}

The combination $(\mu-\mu_0)/\sigma$ keeps appearing, so name it. The **standardized effect
size**

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma}
$$ {#eq-cohen-d}

is the effect measured in units of the noise — "how many standard deviations does the truth
sit from the null." (For the one-sample mean this is Cohen's $d$.) It is dimensionless,
which is exactly the point: a raw effect of "$0.3$ pH units" or "$5$ ms" means nothing until
you know how noisy the measurement is, but $\delta=0.3$ means the same thing — a small,
hard-to-detect effect — in every field. With this, @eq-power becomes the clean

$$
\pi \;=\; \Phi\bigl(\sqrt n\,\delta - z_\alpha\bigr),
$$ {#eq-power-d}

and power depends on the data only through the single product $\sqrt n\,\delta$. Cohen's
rough conventions ($\delta\approx 0.2$ small, $0.5$ medium, $0.8$ large) are field-agnostic
anchors, not laws — what counts as a meaningful $\delta$ is a *subject-matter* judgment,
which is the honest caveat of @sec-when.

## The power curve {#sec-power-curve}

Fix $\delta$, $\sigma$, and $\alpha$; sweep $n$. The graph of power against sample size is
the **power curve**, and it is the single most useful object in experiment design: it tells
you, for a target power (conventionally $0.80$), exactly how much data to buy.
@fig-power-curve draws it for several effect sizes.

```{python}
#| label: fig-power-curve
#| fig-cap: 'Power curves for the one-sided z-test at $\alpha=0.05$, power $\pi=\Phi(\sqrt{n}\,\delta - z_{0.05})$, for four standardized effect sizes. Each curve climbs from $\alpha=0.05$ at $n=0$ (no data, no power beyond the false-alarm floor) toward $1$. The horizontal dashed line marks the conventional target power $0.80$; where a curve crosses it (dots) is the required sample size for that effect. The lesson is the spacing: a large effect ($\delta=0.8$) needs only a handful of observations, while a small effect ($\delta=0.2$) needs nearly 155 — a 16-fold gap from a 4-fold change in $\delta$, the $1/\delta^2$ law made visible.'
z_a = stats.norm.ppf(0.95)        # z_{0.05}, one-sided
ns = np.arange(1, 220)
deltas = [0.2, 0.35, 0.5, 0.8]
colors = [REJECT, TYPE2, NULLC, ALTC]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.4))
for d, col in zip(deltas, colors):
    power = stats.norm.cdf(np.sqrt(ns) * d - z_a)
    ax.plot(ns, power, color=col, label=fr"$\delta={d}$")
    # mark the n that first reaches 0.80
    n_req = (z_a + stats.norm.ppf(0.80)) ** 2 / d ** 2
    ax.plot([n_req], [0.80], "o", color=col, ms=7)
ax.axhline(0.80, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3, label="target power 0.80")
ax.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"power $1-\beta$")
ax.set_title("Power curves: bigger effects need far less data")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.02)
ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
for d in deltas:
    n_req = (z_a + stats.norm.ppf(0.80)) ** 2 / d ** 2
    print(f"delta={d}:  n for 80% power = {np.ceil(n_req):.0f}")
```

The dots are the answer to "how much data do I need," and reading them off the curve is the
graphical version of the algebra we do next.

## Inverting for sample size {#sec-sample-size}

The power curve answers "what power do I get from $n$?" The design question is the inverse:
"what $n$ gives me a target power $1-\beta$?" Solve @eq-power-d for $n$. We want
$\pi = 1-\beta$, so set

$$
1-\beta = \Phi\bigl(\sqrt n\,\delta - z_\alpha\bigr).
$$

Apply $\Phi^{-1}$ to both sides. Define $z_\beta = \Phi^{-1}(1-\beta)$, the upper-$\beta$
quantile (e.g. for power $0.80$, $\beta=0.20$ and $z_{0.20}=0.84$). Then

$$
z_\beta = \Phi^{-1}(1-\beta) = \sqrt n\,\delta - z_\alpha
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sqrt n\,\delta = z_\alpha + z_\beta .
$$

Divide by $\delta$ and square — every step in the open:

$$
\sqrt n = \frac{z_\alpha + z_\beta}{\delta}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\boxed{\; n = \frac{(z_\alpha + z_\beta)^2}{\delta^2}
          = \frac{(z_\alpha + z_\beta)^2\,\sigma^2}{(\mu-\mu_0)^2}. \;}
$$ {#eq-samplesize}

This is the sample-size formula, and three features of it are worth memorizing:

- **The numerator $(z_\alpha+z_\beta)^2$ is the "stringency tax."** It is set by how strict
  you are about *both* errors. Demanding smaller $\alpha$ pushes $z_\alpha$ up; demanding
  higher power pushes $z_\beta$ up; either raises $n$. For the canonical
  one-sided $\alpha=0.05$, power $0.80$: $z_\alpha=1.645$, $z_\beta=0.84$, so
  $(z_\alpha+z_\beta)^2 = (2.49)^2 \approx 6.2$.
- **The denominator $\delta^2$ is the killer.** Because the effect enters *squared*, halving
  $\delta$ quadruples $n$. This is the most important practical fact in the article: small
  effects are expensive, and the cost grows quadratically.
- **It is a floor, not a ceiling.** @eq-samplesize is the *minimum* $n$ for the target
  power; real designs round up and pad for dropout, non-normality, and the t-correction
  below.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"For a two-sided test, what changes?"* Replace $z_\alpha$ with $z_{\alpha/2}$ — the
rejection region splits its budget into two tails, so each cutoff moves out (from $1.645$ to
$1.96$ at $\alpha=0.05$). The sample size becomes
$n = (z_{\alpha/2}+z_\beta)^2/\delta^2$. There is no second $z_\beta/2$: the power is still
governed by one tail (the alternative shifts in one direction, so essentially all the
detection happens in the near tail; the far tail contributes negligibly). The two-sided test
needs slightly more data than the one-sided for the same power — the price of being able to
detect effects in *either* direction. *"And for a two-sample comparison of means?"* The
variance of $\bar X-\bar Y$ is $\sigma^2(1/n_1+1/n_2)$; for equal groups of size $n$ each,
this gives $n = 2(z_{\alpha/2}+z_\beta)^2/\delta^2$ **per group** — the factor of $2$ because
each group's mean carries its own sampling noise.
:::

## Minimum detectable effect {#sec-mde}

Often the budget is fixed first — you have $n=64$ subjects and no more — and the design
question flips again: *what is the smallest effect this study can reliably detect?* Solve
@eq-power-d for $\delta$ at the target power, i.e. rearrange $\sqrt n\,\delta=z_\alpha+z_\beta$:

$$
\boxed{\; \delta_{\min} = \frac{z_\alpha + z_\beta}{\sqrt n}. \;}
$$ {#eq-mde}

This **minimum detectable effect** (MDE) is the honesty check every study should report. If
your fixed-$n$ study can only detect effects of $\delta_{\min}=0.6$ with $80\%$ power, and
the scientifically interesting effects are around $\delta=0.2$, then *a non-significant
result tells you nothing* — the study was never capable of seeing the effect you care about.
The MDE converts a budget into a candid statement of the study's reach, and it falls only as
$1/\sqrt n$: to halve the smallest detectable effect you must quadruple the sample, the same
$1/\delta^2$ law read from the other side.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the machinery on real design questions, and push the last one to the edge where the
naive reading of a "null result" misleads.

## Example 1 — sizing a clinical trial, by hand {#sec-ex-trial}

A team plans a trial of a blood-pressure drug. From prior studies, systolic BP has standard
deviation $\sigma=12$ mmHg, and the smallest *clinically meaningful* reduction is
$\mu-\mu_0=4$ mmHg — anything less is not worth prescribing. They will run a one-sided test
at $\alpha=0.05$ and want $80\%$ power. How many patients?

```{python}
#| label: ex-trial
#| echo: true
sigma = 12.0          # mmHg, known from prior data
raw_effect = 4.0      # mmHg, smallest clinically meaningful reduction
alpha = 0.05
target_power = 0.80

delta = raw_effect / sigma                       # standardized effect (Cohen's d)
z_a = stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha)                  # one-sided z_0.05
z_b = stats.norm.ppf(target_power)               # z_0.20
n_float = (z_a + z_b) ** 2 / delta ** 2
n = int(np.ceil(n_float))

# Verify by plugging back into the power formula.
achieved = stats.norm.cdf(np.sqrt(n) * delta - z_a)
print(f"standardized effect delta = {raw_effect}/{sigma} = {delta:.4f}")
print(f"z_alpha = {z_a:.4f},  z_beta = {z_b:.4f}")
print(f"(z_a + z_b)^2           = {(z_a + z_b)**2:.4f}")
print(f"required n (exact)      = {n_float:.2f}  ->  round up to {n}")
print(f"achieved power at n={n}: {achieved:.4f}  (>= 0.80, as required)")
```

Walk the arithmetic against @eq-samplesize:

- The standardized effect is $\delta = 4/12 = `{python} f"{4/12:.3f}"`$ — a *small* effect by
  Cohen's anchors, which already warns us the study will not be tiny.
- The stringency tax is $(z_\alpha+z_\beta)^2 = (1.645+0.842)^2 = `{python} f"{(stats.norm.ppf(0.95)+stats.norm.ppf(0.80))**2:.2f}"`$.
- So $n = `{python} f"{(stats.norm.ppf(0.95)+stats.norm.ppf(0.80))**2:.2f}"` / `{python} f"{(4/12)**2:.4f}"` = `{python} f"{(stats.norm.ppf(0.95)+stats.norm.ppf(0.80))**2/(4/12)**2:.1f}"`$, rounded up to
  $`{python} f"{int(np.ceil((stats.norm.ppf(0.95)+stats.norm.ppf(0.80))**2/(4/12)**2))}"`$ patients.

The honest design conversation now writes itself. If the team can only recruit $30$
patients, @eq-mde says their MDE is
$\delta_{\min}=(1.645+0.842)/\sqrt{30}=`{python} f"{(stats.norm.ppf(0.95)+stats.norm.ppf(0.80))/np.sqrt(30):.2f}"`$
standardized units, i.e. a raw reduction of
$`{python} f"{12*(stats.norm.ppf(0.95)+stats.norm.ppf(0.80))/np.sqrt(30):.1f}"`$ mmHg —
they could only reliably detect a drug about *35% more* effective than the one worth having.
A $30$-patient trial of this drug is not a smaller version of the right study; it is the
wrong study, and a null result from it would be uninformative. @fig-trial-sensitivity shows
how the required $n$ explodes as the meaningful effect shrinks.

```{python}
#| label: fig-trial-sensitivity
#| fig-cap: 'The $1/\delta^2$ cost curve for the blood-pressure trial ($\sigma=12$, one-sided $\alpha=0.05$, 80% power). Required sample size against the smallest meaningful raw effect. Detecting an 8 mmHg drop needs only ~14 patients; the 4 mmHg drop the team cares about needs ~56; chasing a 2 mmHg drop would need ~223. The curve is the quadratic blow-up of @eq-samplesize — every halving of the target effect quadruples the cost. The vertical line marks the team''s chosen 4 mmHg threshold.'
z_a = stats.norm.ppf(0.95); z_b = stats.norm.ppf(0.80)
effects = np.linspace(1.5, 9, 200)
n_needed = (z_a + z_b) ** 2 * sigma ** 2 / effects ** 2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
ax.plot(effects, n_needed, color=NULLC)
for e in (8, 4, 2):
    nn = (z_a + z_b) ** 2 * sigma ** 2 / e ** 2
    ax.plot([e], [nn], "o", color=REJECT, ms=7)
    ax.annotate(f"{e} mmHg: n={np.ceil(nn):.0f}", xy=(e, nn),
                xytext=(e + 0.3, nn + 18), fontsize=9,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=GREY, lw=1.0))
ax.axvline(4.0, color=ALTC, ls="--", lw=1.3, label="chosen 4 mmHg threshold")
ax.set_xlabel("smallest meaningful effect (mmHg)")
ax.set_ylabel("required sample size $n$")
ax.set_title("Sample size explodes as the target effect shrinks")
ax.set_ylim(0, 260); ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 2 — significance is not importance, sized two ways {#sec-effect-vs-sig}

[INFR-D1]'s Trap 3 said a tiny effect becomes significant with enough data. Power analysis
makes that precise and turns it into a design discipline: it forces you to declare the
*smallest effect worth detecting* in advance, so you neither under- nor over-collect. We
show both failure directions on one canvas — a study too small to see a real effect, and a
study so large it will flag a meaningless one.

```{python}
#| label: fig-effect-vs-sig
#| fig-cap: 'Two failures of mismatched sample size, one-sided $\alpha=0.05$. **Left:** power against the *true* standardized effect for three sample sizes. A genuinely meaningful effect ($\delta=0.5$, green line) is caught 94% of the time at $n=40$ but under 48% of the time at $n=10$ — the small study misses a real effect more often than not. **Right:** the same curves read for a *trivial* effect ($\delta=0.1$, red line): the huge $n=400$ study has 64% power to flag it as significant, even though $\delta=0.1$ is practically nothing. Power analysis is the discipline that matches $n$ to the effect that matters — big enough to catch $\delta=0.5$, not so big it chases $\delta=0.1$.'
z_a = stats.norm.ppf(0.95)
d_grid = np.linspace(0, 1.0, 300)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 3.9), sharey=True)

for n_s, col in [(10, REJECT), (40, NULLC), (400, ALTC)]:
    pw = stats.norm.cdf(np.sqrt(n_s) * d_grid - z_a)
    axL.plot(d_grid, pw, color=col, label=f"n={n_s}")
    axR.plot(d_grid, pw, color=col, label=f"n={n_s}")

axL.axvline(0.5, color=ALTC, ls="--", lw=1.2)
axL.set_title("A real effect ($\\delta=0.5$) can be missed")
axL.set_xlabel(r"true effect size $\delta$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"power")
axL.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.axvline(0.1, color=REJECT, ls="--", lw=1.2)
axR.set_xlim(0, 0.35)
axR.set_title("A trivial effect ($\\delta=0.1$) can be chased")
axR.set_xlabel(r"true effect size $\delta$")
axR.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"power for delta=0.5 at n=10:  {stats.norm.cdf(np.sqrt(10)*0.5 - z_a):.3f}")
print(f"power for delta=0.5 at n=40:  {stats.norm.cdf(np.sqrt(40)*0.5 - z_a):.3f}")
print(f"power for delta=0.1 at n=400: {stats.norm.cdf(np.sqrt(400)*0.1 - z_a):.3f}")
```

The two panels are the same formula read for opposite mistakes. Under-power ($n=10$ chasing
$\delta=0.5$) and over-power ($n=400$ chasing $\delta=0.1$) are both *design* failures, and
both are prevented by the same act: deciding, before data, the effect size that matters and
sizing the study to it. The power calculation is precisely the tool that pins down "how big
is big enough" so neither tail of @fig-effect-vs-sig catches you.

## Example 3 — the t-test correction and a simulation check {#sec-ex-ttest}

Real studies almost never know $\sigma$; [INFR-D2] replaced it with the sample $s$, turning
the z-test into the t-test. Power changes in two linked ways, and both push the same
direction — you need a *little more* data than the z-formula says:

1. The null distribution is $t_{n-1}$, with **heavier tails** than the Normal ([INFR-D2]
   @fig-t-vs-z), so the critical value is larger ($t_{n-1,\alpha}>z_\alpha$) — a higher bar.
2. Under the alternative the statistic follows a **noncentral $t$** with noncentrality
   $\lambda=\sqrt n\,\delta$, not a shifted Normal, because the random denominator $s$ adds
   its own variability.

The exact power is $\pi = P\bigl(T'_{n-1,\lambda} \ge t_{n-1,\alpha}\bigr)$, where
$T'_{n-1,\lambda}$ is noncentral-$t$. The clean approximation in practice is to use the
sample-size formula @eq-samplesize and add a few to $n$ (or solve the noncentral-$t$ power
numerically, which `scipy` does directly). The gap is small and vanishes as $n$ grows,
because $t_{n-1}\to\mathcal N(0,1)$. We verify the whole apparatus by **simulating** the
t-test thousands of times at the designed $n$ and counting how often it rejects — the
empirical power should land on the formula.

```{python}
#| label: fig-power-sim
#| fig-cap: 'The power formula, validated by brute-force simulation of the one-sample t-test (one-sided, $\alpha=0.05$, true standardized effect $\delta=0.5$). For each sample size we draw 4000 datasets from $\mathcal{N}(\delta,1)$, run the t-test, and record the fraction that reject (dots). The solid line is the exact noncentral-$t$ power; the dashed line is the simpler Normal approximation $\Phi(\sqrt{n}\,\delta - z_\alpha)$. All three agree closely, and the small gap between the dashed Normal curve and the solid $t$ curve at small $n$ is exactly the price of estimating $\sigma$ — the t-test needs slightly more data for the same power. The simulation confirms the derived formula describes a real, runnable test.'
delta = 0.5
alpha = 0.05
ns = np.array([5, 8, 12, 18, 25, 35, 50])
reps = 4000

emp_power = []
for n_s in ns:
    rejects = 0
    tcrit = stats.t.ppf(1 - alpha, df=n_s - 1)     # one-sided t critical value
    for _ in range(reps):
        s = rng.normal(delta, 1.0, size=n_s)        # sigma = 1, true mean = delta
        tstat = s.mean() / (s.std(ddof=1) / np.sqrt(n_s))
        if tstat >= tcrit:
            rejects += 1
    emp_power.append(rejects / reps)
emp_power = np.array(emp_power)

# Exact noncentral-t power and the Normal approximation.
nn = np.arange(3, 55)
z_a = stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha)
exact = np.array([1 - stats.nct.cdf(stats.t.ppf(1 - alpha, df=k - 1),
                                    df=k - 1, nc=np.sqrt(k) * delta) for k in nn])
approx = stats.norm.cdf(np.sqrt(nn) * delta - z_a)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.3))
ax.plot(nn, exact, color=NULLC, label="exact noncentral-$t$ power")
ax.plot(nn, approx, color=ALTC, ls="--", label=r"Normal approx $\Phi(\sqrt{n}\,\delta - z_\alpha)$")
ax.plot(ns, emp_power, "o", color=REJECT, ms=7, label="simulated t-test")
ax.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"power $1-\beta$")
ax.set_title("Derived power matches a simulated t-test ($\\delta=0.5$)")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.02); ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
for n_s, ep in zip(ns, emp_power):
    print(f"n={n_s:>3}: simulated power = {ep:.3f}")
```

The dots sit on the curves: the derivation is not an abstraction, it predicts how often a
real, $\sigma$-unknown t-test will fire. And the modest gap between the dashed (Normal) and
solid (noncentral-$t$) curves at small $n$ is the honest correction — at $n=5$ the t-test
has visibly less power than the z-formula promises, because estimating $\sigma$ from $4$
degrees of freedom costs you.

# The edge of applicability: underpowered is not "no effect" {#sec-underpowered}

This is the section that separates "can compute power" from "won't be fooled by a null
result," and it is the most consequential confusion in applied statistics.

[INFR-D1] hammered that *failing to reject $H_0$ is not accepting $H_0$*. Power analysis
explains exactly *why* and quantifies *when* a null result is worthless. If a study's power
against the effect you care about is low — say $0.30$ — then **even when the effect is
real, the study fails to detect it $70\%$ of the time.** A non-significant result from such
a study is the *expected* outcome whether or not the effect exists, so it discriminates
nothing. "We found no significant effect" from an underpowered study is not evidence the
effect is absent; it is evidence the study could not see.

```{python}
#| label: fig-underpowered
#| fig-cap: 'Why a null result from an underpowered study is uninformative. We simulate a world where the effect is *genuinely real* ($\delta=0.3$), running a one-sided $\alpha=0.05$ t-test 5000 times at each sample size, and plot the fraction of studies that *fail* to reach significance ($p>0.05$). At $n=15$ the real effect is missed about 70% of the time; even at $n=50$ it is missed roughly a third of the time. A single ''not significant'' from a small study is the *most likely* outcome even though the effect exists — so it cannot be read as ''no effect.'' The dashed line is the implied power $1-\beta$; the miss rate is its complement $\beta$. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence, made quantitative.'
delta_true = 0.3
alpha = 0.05
ns = np.array([15, 25, 40, 50, 80, 120, 200])
reps = 5000

miss_rate = []
for n_s in ns:
    tcrit = stats.t.ppf(1 - alpha, df=n_s - 1)
    fails = 0
    for _ in range(reps):
        s = rng.normal(delta_true, 1.0, size=n_s)
        tstat = s.mean() / (s.std(ddof=1) / np.sqrt(n_s))
        if tstat < tcrit:        # failed to reject, despite real effect
            fails += 1
    miss_rate.append(fails / reps)
miss_rate = np.array(miss_rate)

z_a = stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha)
nn = np.arange(10, 210)
power_curve = stats.norm.cdf(np.sqrt(nn) * delta_true - z_a)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.3))
ax.plot(ns, miss_rate, "o-", color=REJECT, label=r"P(miss a REAL effect) = $\beta$")
ax.plot(nn, 1 - power_curve, color=TYPE2, ls=":", lw=1.6, label=r"theoretical $\beta = 1 - \Phi(\sqrt{n}\delta - z_\alpha)$")
ax.plot(nn, power_curve, color=ALTC, ls="--", lw=1.4, label=r"power $1-\beta$")
ax.axhline(0.5, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
ax.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); ax.set_ylabel("probability")
ax.set_title("A real effect ($\\delta=0.3$) is routinely missed when underpowered")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.02); ax.legend(loc="center right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
for n_s, mr in zip(ns, miss_rate):
    print(f"n={n_s:>3}: P(fail to detect real effect) = {mr:.3f}")
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A non-significant result only carries information if the study had the power to detect the
effect you care about.** Always report the study's power (or its minimum detectable effect,
@eq-mde) alongside a null result. "We did not detect an effect, and our study had $85\%$
power to detect an effect of $\delta=0.4$" is a real, near-conclusive negative finding: had
a meaningful effect existed, we very likely would have seen it. "We did not detect an effect"
from a study with $20\%$ power is *no finding at all* — it is a coin flip dressed as a
conclusion. Equating "not significant" with "no effect," without checking power, is the
single most common way underpowered studies are over-interpreted.
:::

## The winner's curse and the file drawer {#sec-winners-curse}

Underpowering does more than hide effects; it *corrupts the ones it finds*. When power is
low, the only samples that clear the significance bar are the ones where chance happened to
inflate the estimate — so **the published, significant estimates are systematically too
large.** This is the *winner's curse* of statistics. Combine it with the **file-drawer
problem** (non-significant studies go unpublished) and **p-hacking** (analyzing many ways
until something crosses $0.05$), and a literature of underpowered studies can be both
*statistically significant on average* and *substantively wrong* — the [INFR-D1]
base-rate trap, now driven by low power.

```{python}
#| label: fig-winners-curse
#| fig-cap: 'The winner''s curse. We simulate many low-power studies of a real but modest effect ($\delta=0.3$, $n=20$, one-sided $\alpha=0.05$, power $\approx 0.35$). The histogram shows the estimated effect $\hat\delta=\bar X/s$ across all studies (grey) versus only the *significant* ones (red). The true effect is the dashed line. Among significant studies the average estimate (red line) is roughly double the truth: significance, at low power, is a filter that admits only the luckily-inflated samples. A literature built from significant underpowered studies systematically overstates effects.'
delta_true = 0.3
n_s = 20
alpha = 0.05
reps = 8000
tcrit = stats.t.ppf(1 - alpha, df=n_s - 1)

est_all, est_sig = [], []
for _ in range(reps):
    s = rng.normal(delta_true, 1.0, size=n_s)
    dhat = s.mean() / s.std(ddof=1)
    tstat = s.mean() / (s.std(ddof=1) / np.sqrt(n_s))
    est_all.append(dhat)
    if tstat >= tcrit:
        est_sig.append(dhat)
est_all = np.array(est_all); est_sig = np.array(est_sig)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.3))
ax.hist(est_all, bins=45, color=FILL, alpha=0.7, label="all studies")
ax.hist(est_sig, bins=25, color=REJECT, alpha=0.6, label="only significant studies")
ax.axvline(delta_true, color=NULLC, ls="--", lw=1.8, label=fr"true $\delta={delta_true}$")
ax.axvline(est_sig.mean(), color=REJECT, lw=1.8,
           label=fr"mean of significant $\hat\delta={est_sig.mean():.2f}$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"estimated effect $\hat\delta$"); ax.set_ylabel("count")
ax.set_title("Low power inflates the effects that reach significance")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"true delta = {delta_true}")
print(f"mean estimate, all studies        = {est_all.mean():.3f}")
print(f"mean estimate, significant studies = {est_sig.mean():.3f}  (inflated)")
print(f"empirical power = {len(est_sig)/reps:.3f}")
```

The red histogram's mean sits far above the dashed truth: the significant subset is a biased
sample of all studies, selected for inflated estimates. This is why "well-powered" is not
bureaucratic box-ticking — underpowered significant findings are *expected* to overstate
effects, and a field that publishes them builds a distorted record.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is post-hoc (observed) power, and why is it useless?"* It is power computed *after*
the study using the *observed* effect as if it were the true effect. It is worthless — even
actively misleading — for two reasons. First, it is a deterministic, one-to-one function of
the p-value (a non-significant result *always* yields low observed power), so it adds no
information beyond the p-value you already have. Second, it answers the wrong question:
power is a property of a design against a *hypothesized* effect you choose in advance, used
to *plan*; computing it from the noisy observed effect just feeds the estimate's own error
back into the calculation. The legitimate retrospective tool is to report the power the
study had against the *smallest meaningful* effect (or the MDE), fixed independently of what
you observed — never the observed-effect power.
:::

# When is power analysis the right tool? {#sec-when}

The article would be dishonest without an explicit applicability verdict.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When do you do a power analysis, and what are its limits?"* The summary worth reciting:

- **Always, before a confirmatory study,** to choose $n$ (@eq-samplesize) or to state the
  MDE for a fixed budget (@eq-mde). A confirmatory study with no power plan risks being
  unable to answer its own question — the silent failure of @sec-why.
- **Its hardest input is the effect size you plug in.** Power analysis is only as honest as
  the $\delta$ you feed it, and that $\delta$ should be the *smallest effect worth
  detecting* (a subject-matter judgment), **not** a guess at the true effect and emphatically
  **not** a pilot study's noisy estimate (which inherits the winner's curse and gives an
  over-optimistic, too-small $n$). Plug in the effect that would *matter*, not the one you
  hope is there.
- **It assumes the test's model.** The clean formulas here are for Normal means; real designs
  with binary outcomes, survival times, clustered or repeated measures, or unequal group
  sizes need the matching power calculation (often done by simulation, exactly as in
  @fig-power-sim — simulate the actual analysis at candidate $n$ and count rejections). When
  the parametric model is doubtful, simulate; do not force a formula.
- **Do not use observed (post-hoc) power** to interpret a completed study (@sec-winners-curse).
- **And the deepest point:** power analysis governs the **Type II** side that the rest of
  [INFR-D1]'s apparatus ignored. It does not replace effect sizes and confidence intervals —
  it *complements* them: power decides whether the study can see, the CI reports what it saw
  and how precisely. Under multiplicity ([INFR-D5]) the bar $\alpha$ tightens, which *lowers*
  power and *raises* the required $n$ — power and multiple testing are coupled, which is the
  next article's concern.
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You would not evaluate
$\Phi$ and noncentral-$t$ tails by hand. In Python, `statsmodels.stats.power` provides
`TTestPower`, `TTestIndPower`, `NormalIndPower`, and `GofChisquarePower`, each with a
`.power(...)` method and a `.solve_power(...)` that inverts for whichever of
$\{n, \delta, \alpha, \text{power}\}$ you leave as `None` — exactly the four-levers
relationship of the Core, solved for any one. The R ecosystem's `pwr` package and the
standalone `G*Power` tool do the same. For any analysis without a closed-form power (logistic
regression, mixed models, custom pipelines), the **simulation recipe of @fig-power-sim is
the universal fallback**: assume the effect, generate data at a candidate $n$, run your
actual analysis, repeat thousands of times, and the rejection fraction *is* the power. The
formula @eq-samplesize is what these tools compute for the Normal-mean case; you now know
what they are doing and why $n\propto 1/\delta^2$.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-D1]**, which defined power as $1-\beta$ but spent its discipline on
the null side, and **[INFR-D2]**, which built the z- and t-test statistics. This article
turned [INFR-D1]'s $\beta$ into a *design tool*: the power function (@def-power), the explicit
power formula @eq-power, the sample-size law @eq-samplesize with its decisive $1/\delta^2$
scaling, and the honest boundary between underpowered and "no effect." From here:

- **[INFR-D5 *Multiple Comparisons*]** — when you run many tests, controlling family-wise
  error tightens each test's effective $\alpha$, which *cuts power* and *inflates* the
  required $n$. Power and multiplicity are the two halves of designing a study that screens
  many hypotheses without either drowning in false alarms or missing every real effect.
- **[INFR-D3 *The Asymptotic Trinity*]** — the Neyman–Pearson lemma there identifies the
  *most powerful* test for a given $\alpha$, the theoretical ceiling on the power this
  article computed for the z/t test specifically.
- **[INFR-C1]/[INFR-C2]** — the confidence interval is power's complement at analysis time:
  by the [INFR-D1] duality, a study powered to detect $\delta$ is one whose CI will be narrow
  enough to exclude $\mu_0$ when the effect is real. Reporting the CI shows the effect size
  that power was sized against.
- **Downstream in Regression** — every designed experiment (A/B tests, RCTs, dose-response
  studies) and every "how many observations do I need to detect a coefficient of this size"
  question is this article's formula applied to a regression slope.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding decision framework.** J. Neyman & E. S. Pearson, "On the Problem of the Most
  Efficient Tests of Statistical Hypotheses," *Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A* 231 (1933) — where
  power enters testing as the quantity to maximize.
- **The practical engineering of power.** Jacob Cohen, *Statistical Power Analysis for the
  Behavioral Sciences* (2nd ed., 1988) — standardized effect sizes, power tables, and the
  case that whole fields were chronically underpowered. His 1962 survey of the abnormal-
  psychology literature first sounded the alarm.
- **The modern crisis literature.** Button et al., "Power failure: why small sample size
  undermines the reliability of neuroscience," *Nature Reviews Neuroscience* 14 (2013), for
  the winner's curse and replication damage of @sec-winners-curse; J. Ioannidis, "Why Most
  Published Research Findings Are False," *PLoS Medicine* 2 (2005), for the base-rate-plus-
  low-power mechanism.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *How does multiplicity change the required $n$?* —
  [INFR-D5]. *Is there a most powerful test, and what is its power ceiling?* — the
  Neyman–Pearson lemma, [INFR-D3]. *What about power for non-Normal or complex models?* —
  simulate the analysis (@fig-power-sim); the principle is unchanged, only the null and
  alternative distributions differ.
